criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the VALID algorithm framework, including atomic certificates, domain certificates, and the log-ratio based rejection sampling mechanism.
The agent has read Section 2.1 to understand the VALID inference procedure (Algorithm 1) and the certified upper bound on output probabilities (Equation 1).
The agent has read Section 3 completely to understand the PRISM framework, including its four loss function variants (GA, NPO, DPO, BC) and their respective training objectives.

The agent has read Section 5.1 to understand the training setup including dataset curation, guide model initialization with LLaMA-3.2-1B, QLoRA fine-tuning, and threshold selection via Youden's J statistic.
The agent has read Appendix B to understand the detailed preprocessing procedures for OOD training data (SQuAD, ShareGPT, Megpie-Llama) including filtering criteria and final dataset sizes.
The agent has read Appendix C and Table 5 to understand the full training configuration, including QLoRA setup, AdamW optimizer, and all variant-specific hyperparameter values.
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the expected results for known OOD evaluation on both ground truth and generated responses.
The agent has read Table 2 to understand the expected F1 and AUC results on two unseen OOD datasets (LMSYS-Chat-1M and WildChat).

The agent has read Appendices F, H, I, and J to understand additional experiments on math OOD, Shakespeare domain generalization, beta sensitivity analysis, and the Qwen3-0.6B alternative guide model.

The plan includes implementing the VALID inference algorithm (Algorithm 1) with rejection sampling, the length-normalized log-ratio threshold check, and the T-attempt loop returning 'Abstained' if no sample is accepted.
The plan includes collecting and preprocessing in-domain data from PubMedQA (20k training samples and 1k validation samples) following the same methodology as Emde et al. (2025).
The plan includes collecting and preprocessing out-of-domain data from SQuAD (5.8k), ShareGPT (6.8k), and Megpie-Llama (6.8k) for a total of 20k OOD training samples.
The plan includes implementing all four PRISM loss functions (GA, NPO, DPO, BC) as described in Section 3.2 with their respective training objectives.
The plan includes initializing the guide model G from LLaMA-3.2-1B pretrained weights and applying QLoRA fine-tuning with approximately 5.6 million trainable parameters.
The plan includes threshold selection using Youden's J statistic (J = TPR - FPR) on the validation set, choosing k* to maximize the balance between OOD detection and preservation of valid responses.
The plan includes implementing all evaluation metrics: FPR, TPR, F1, AUC, and the Median Constriction Ratio (Median CR) as defined in Section 5.3.
The plan includes evaluation on known OOD data with two test scenarios: ground truth responses and responses generated by LLaMA-3-8B.

The plan includes all VALID baselines: naive LLaMA-3.2-1B (no fine-tuning), VALID(200k) using the publicly released checkpoint, and VALID variants re-trained on 5k, 10k, 15k, and 20k in-domain samples.
The plan includes evaluation on two unseen OOD datasets: LMSYS-Chat-1M and WildChat (1k medical-filtered samples from each).
The plan includes constructing the perturbed medical misinformation test set (inverted PubMedQA answers with LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct-generated false explanations) for robustness evaluation.
The plan includes certified benchmarking on the PubMedQA test set at certification strengths epsilon = 10^-5 and epsilon = 10^-10.
The plan includes variant-specific hyperparameter settings from Table 5: epochs (BC: 2, GA: 1, NPO: 5, DPO: 5), warmup (BC: 500 steps, GA/NPO/DPO: 0.1 ratio), and beta (NPO/DPO: 0.1).

The VALID inference algorithm (Algorithm 1) is implemented: for T attempts, sample y ~ L(·|x), compute length-normalized log-ratio log(L(y|x)/G(y))/N_y, accept if <= k, otherwise try again; return 'Abstained' after T failures.
The certified upper bound from Equation 1 is implemented: M_{L,G,k,T}(y|x) <= 2^(k*N_y) * T * G(y), and used to compute the epsilon-DC certification bound.
The standard cross-entropy loss for in-domain fine-tuning (Equation 2: L_CE = -E_{D_T}[log G_theta(y)]) has been implemented.

The PRISM-GA loss function (Equation 3: L_GA = L_CE + E_{D_F}[log G_theta(y)]) has been implemented, combining cross-entropy on in-domain data with gradient ascent (negative cross-entropy) on out-of-domain data.

The PRISM-NPO loss function (Equation 4: L_NPO = (2/beta)*E_{D_F}[log(1 + (G_theta(y)/pi_ref(y))^beta)] + L_CE) has been implemented with a frozen reference model pi_ref and regularization hyperparameter beta.

The PRISM-DPO loss function (Equation 5: L_DPO = -E[log sigma(beta*log(pi_theta(y_w)/pi_ref(y_w)) - beta*log(pi_theta(y_l)/pi_ref(y_l)))]) has been implemented with in-domain samples as preferred (y_w) and out-of-domain samples as dispreferred (y_l).

The PRISM-BC loss function (Equation 6: L_BC = -E[z*sigma(G_theta(y)) + (1-z)*log(1-sigma(G_theta(y)))]) has been implemented as binary cross-entropy with domain indicator z=1 for in-domain and z=0 for out-of-domain.
The PRISM-BC model architecture has been implemented by attaching a randomly initialized regression/classification head to the pretrained LLaMA-3.2-1B to produce sequence-level domain scores.
The guide model G is initialized from LLaMA-3.2-1B pretrained weights (not trained from scratch) for all non-BC PRISM variants, with no architectural modifications.
QLoRA (Quantized Low-Rank Adaptation) fine-tuning is implemented for training the guide model G, reducing trainable parameters to approximately 5.6 million.
The AdamW optimizer with linear learning rate decay is implemented using learning rate 1e-5, batch size 8, and gradient accumulation steps of 4 for all PRISM variants.
The frozen reference model pi_ref is implemented for PRISM-NPO and PRISM-DPO using the initial pretrained LLaMA-3.2-1B weights before fine-tuning, to prevent catastrophic collapse.
The in-domain training data is loaded from PubMedQA, randomly sampling 20k QA pairs from the full 200k dataset using the same preprocessing as Emde et al. (2025).
The SQuAD out-of-domain data is preprocessed: medical topics are removed, responses shorter than 10 tokens are filtered out, resulting in 5.8k QA pairs from the original 98.2k samples.
The ShareGPT out-of-domain data is preprocessed: only the first prompt-response pair from each conversation is extracted, incomplete conversations are removed, resulting in 6.8k entries.
The Megpie-Llama out-of-domain data is preprocessed: medical-related entries are filtered using the VALID model classifier, resulting in 6.8k samples from the synthetic Llama-3.1-based dataset.
The combined OOD training dataset D_F is constructed by aggregating all three sources (SQuAD 5.8k + ShareGPT 6.8k + Megpie-Llama 6.8k) to a total of 20k samples.
A held-out validation set is constructed with 1,000 in-domain PubMedQA samples and 1,000 out-of-domain samples (aggregated from the three OOD sources), disjoint from both training and test data.
Threshold selection is implemented by evaluating Youden's J statistic (J = TPR - FPR) across candidate k values on the validation set, choosing k* that maximizes J.
The naive LLaMA baseline is implemented using LLaMA-3.2-1B with no fine-tuning, computing the log-ratio score against LLaMA-3-8B for domain classification.
The VALID(200k) baseline is implemented using the publicly released VALID checkpoint trained on the full 200k-sample PubMedQA dataset.
VALID variants trained on subsets of 5k, 10k, 15k, and 20k in-domain samples are implemented from scratch following the same methodology as Emde et al. (2025).
Standard binary classification metrics (FPR, TPR, F1, AUC) are implemented to evaluate each model's domain discrimination performance.
The Median Constriction Ratio (Median CR) metric is implemented as the median of epsilon_y(L) / epsilon_y(M) over OOD test samples, where epsilon_y denotes response acceptance probability.
The known OOD ground truth test set is implemented using 1,000 held-out examples drawn i.i.d. from the same distribution as training/validation but not seen during training.
The known OOD generated responses test set is implemented by using LLaMA-3-8B to generate responses to the same prompts as the ground truth test set, introducing a distributional shift.
The unseen OOD evaluation using LMSYS-Chat-1M is implemented by loading the dataset, filtering out medical-related conversations, and sampling 1k conversations.
The unseen OOD evaluation using WildChat is implemented by loading the dataset, filtering out medical-related conversations, and sampling 1k conversations.
The FPR vs. log10(epsilon)-DC trade-off curve is implemented by sweeping the threshold k and recording the resulting FPR and epsilon bound for each model.

The perturbed medical misinformation test set is constructed by inverting correct answers in PubMedQA and prompting LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct to generate convincing but factually false medical explanations.

The certified benchmarking evaluation is implemented: for each epsilon level, a response from LLaMA-3-8B is counted as correct only if it both passes certification by the guide model G AND matches the ground-truth PubMedQA answer.
Variant-specific epoch settings are implemented: PRISM-BC trains for 2 epochs, PRISM-GA for 1 epoch, PRISM-NPO for 5 epochs, and PRISM-DPO for 5 epochs.
Variant-specific warmup settings are implemented: PRISM-BC uses 500 warmup steps while PRISM-GA, PRISM-NPO, and PRISM-DPO use a 0.1 ratio warmup.
The regularization hyperparameter beta is set to 0.1 for both PRISM-NPO and PRISM-DPO in the main experiments, with weight decay 0.01 for all variants.
The math OOD test set (Appendix F.1) is constructed with 900 math-related question samples to evaluate models' ability to reject mathematical content.
The math-in-medical-framing OOD test set (Appendix F.2) is constructed with 100 math problems disguised as medical clinical vignettes, generated by prompting LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct.

The Shakespeare domain generalization experiment (Appendix H) is implemented following the same experimental protocol as VALID (Emde et al., 2025), with appropriate in-domain and out-of-domain data for that domain.
The beta sensitivity analysis (Appendix I) is implemented by training PRISM-DPO with beta values in {0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5} and evaluating each on the medical scenario.
The alternative guide model experiment (Appendix J) is implemented by replacing LLaMA-3.2-1B with Qwen3-0.6B and training PRISM-BC and PRISM-GA with the same settings.
The T > 1 experiments (Appendix G) are implemented for VALID(200k), PRISM-GA, PRISM-BC, PRISM-NPO, and PRISM-DPO, plotting FPR and epsilon bounds for T = 1, 2, 3, 4.

The log-likelihood ratio visualization (Appendix E, Figure 2) is implemented by plotting histograms of normalized log-likelihood ratios for in-domain and out-of-domain test samples across all six model variants.
The training code for all four PRISM variants (GA, NPO, DPO, BC) on the medical QA setup has been executed successfully without errors on the designated hardware (Nvidia A100 40GB).
The known OOD evaluation has been executed successfully for all models on both ground truth and generated response test sets.
The unseen OOD evaluation has been executed successfully for all models on LMSYS-Chat-1M and WildChat datasets.
The perturbed medical misinformation evaluation has been executed successfully for all models on the constructed challenging test set.
The certified benchmarking on PubMedQA has been executed successfully for all models at both epsilon = 10^-5 and epsilon = 10^-10.
The VALID data scaling experiments have been executed successfully for VALID variants trained on 5k, 10k, 15k, 20k, and 200k in-domain samples (Appendix D).
The additional experiments in Appendices F.1, F.2, H, and J (math OOD, math-in-medical-framing, Shakespeare, and Qwen3-0.6B guide) have been executed successfully without errors.
The execution outcomes match Table 1 (known OOD, ground truth responses): PRISM-BC achieves FPR=0.001, TPR=1.0, F1=1.0, Median CR=3217.34, AUC=1.0; PRISM-GA achieves the largest Median CR (14997.20).
The execution outcomes match Table 1 (known OOD, generated responses): PRISM-GA achieves the highest Median CR (8544.93) and all PRISM variants outperform VALID on FPR and AUC.

The execution outcomes confirm that all PRISM variants consistently outperform VALID models in Table 1, with PRISM models achieving lower FPR and higher TPR, F1, and AUC than both VALID(20k) and VALID(200k).

The execution outcomes match Table 2 (unseen OOD): PRISM-BC achieves F1=0.994, AUC=0.999 on LMSYS-Chat-1M and F1=0.994, AUC=0.998 on WildChat; PRISM-GA achieves F1=0.982, AUC=0.996 and F1=0.987, AUC=0.996 respectively.

The execution outcomes match the FPR vs. epsilon-DC trade-off shown in Figure 1 (LMSYS-Chat): only PRISM-GA and PRISM-BC achieve FPR below 20% within the meaningful certification range (log10(epsilon) <= 0), while VALID(200k) exceeds 50% FPR throughout.

The execution outcomes match Table 3 (perturbed misinformation): PRISM-BC achieves the best performance (F1=0.799, AUC=0.924, Median CR=6.14) and VALID models are outperformed by the naive LLaMA baseline (F1=0.724).

The execution outcomes match Table 4 (certified benchmarking on PubMedQA): PRISM-BC maintains accuracy 0.724 at both epsilon=10^-5 and epsilon=10^-10, while VALID(200k) drops to 0.318 and 0.004 respectively, against an unconstrained baseline of 72.5%.

The execution outcomes match Table 8 (math OOD, Appendix F.1): PRISM-BC achieves perfect detection (FPR=0.0, TPR=1.0, F1=1.0) and PRISM-GA achieves FPR=0.0 with F1=0.999, both substantially outperforming VALID(200k) (F1=0.937).

The execution outcomes match Table 9 (math-in-medical-framing, Appendix F.2): PRISM-BC achieves perfect detection (F1=1.0, Median CR=444.20) and PRISM-GA achieves F1=0.971, while VALID models fail with F1 below 0.5.

The execution outcomes match Table 10 (Shakespeare domain, Appendix H): PRISM-BC achieves F1=1.0 and FPR=0.0, PRISM-GA achieves Median CR=14061.69 compared to VALID's 206.33, confirming transferability to non-factual domains.

The execution outcomes match Table 11 (beta sensitivity, Appendix I): beta=0.1 achieves the best detection F1 (0.979) but lower Median CR (47.60); beta=0.001 achieves the highest Median CR (892.22) but slightly higher FPR (0.037), confirming the detection-robustness trade-off.

The execution outcomes match Table 12 (Qwen3-0.6B guide, Appendix J): PRISM-BC achieves F1 > 0.999 with FPR=0.001, and PRISM-GA achieves Median CR=25,830.55, confirming architecture-agnostic effectiveness.

The execution outcomes confirm VALID data scaling behavior from Appendix D (Tables 6-7): VALID's performance does not scale monotonically with training data across 5k to 200k in-domain samples for either ground truth or generated response evaluations.
